narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law program was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students with the strongest projected odds of bar passage success. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, which stood out as the biggest factors pushing the model toward expecting a failure. Specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1—meaning you ranked in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—had the strongest negative influence, far below the typical student's mid-range position around the 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 decile of 1 similarly pulled the prediction downward compared to the average applicant's stronger start. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA above the typical 3.21 average worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success. Your low family income bracket also helped slightly, as the model sees resilience in such backgrounds among successful bar takers. Your LSAT score of 27.50 didn't sway things much either way, landing below the dataset average of about 37, while your full-time enrollment plans offered a small positive boost, aligning with the vast majority of students who pass. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who boast higher early deciles and stronger test scores, your profile highlighted those early academic struggles as the key risks. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings in future coursework, and perhaps exploring programs with bridge support for students from low-income backgrounds. You're capable of turning this around; consider reapplying after addressing these areas, and feel free to reach out for personalized guidance. Keep pushing—you've got this.
